title: Die sieben Raben
about: The twenty-fifth tale (KHM 25): seven brothers are cursed into ravens over a christening errand, and their little sister journeys to the ends of the earth — sun, moon, and stars — to redeem them. Spelling follows the 1857 edition (Thor, Thüre, gieng, ward).
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 25. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.

sentences.t: Ein Mann hatte sieben Söhne und immer noch kein Töchterchen, so sehr er sichs auch wünschte; endlich gab ihm seine Frau wieder gute Hoffnung zu einem Kinde, und wies zur Welt kam, wars auch ein Mädchen.
sentences.l: A man had seven sons and still no little daughter, however much he wished it for himself; at last his wife gave him again good hope of a child, and when it came into the world, it too was a girl.
sentences.i: A man had seven sons and still no little daughter, however much he longed for one; at last his wife once more gave him good hope of a child, and when it came into the world, it too proved to be a girl.
sentences.n: “sichs” = sich es (‘wished it for himself’); likewise “wies” = wie es and “wars” = war es — the 1857 text glues these little pronoun contractions on with an s. “gute Hoffnung” of a coming child is an old idiom for being with child. “zu einem Kinde” has the old dative -e.

sentences.t: Die Freude war groß, aber das Kind war schmächtig und klein, und sollte wegen seiner Schwachheit die Nothtaufe haben.
sentences.l: The joy was great, but the child was frail and small, and was to have the emergency baptism because of its weakness.
sentences.i: The joy was great, but the child was puny and small, and was to be given an emergency baptism on account of its feebleness.
sentences.n: “Nothtaufe” (1857 -th-; modern Nottaufe) is a hasty baptism performed when a newborn is not expected to live. “seiner” points back to das Kind (neuter), hence ‘its.’ “wegen” takes the genitive.

sentences.t: Der Vater schickte einen der Knaben eilends zur Quelle, Taufwasser zu holen: die andern sechs liefen mit und weil jeder der erste beim Schöpfen sein wollte, so fiel ihnen der Krug in den Brunnen.
sentences.l: The father sent one of the boys in haste to the spring, to fetch baptismal water: the other six ran along, and because each wanted to be the first at the drawing, the jug fell for them into the well.
sentences.i: The father sent one of the boys in haste to the spring to fetch baptismal water; the other six ran along, and because each wanted to be first at drawing it, the jug fell into the well.
sentences.n: “liefen mit” (mitlaufen, to run along) is separable. “beim Schöpfen” — beim + a nominalized infinitive marks an activity under way (‘at the drawing of water’). “fiel ihnen der Krug” has a dative of disadvantage: the jug fell on them, to their loss.

sentences.t: Da standen sie und wußten nicht was sie thun sollten, und keiner getraute sich heim.
sentences.l: There they stood and knew not what they should do, and none dared [to go] home.
sentences.i: There they stood, not knowing what to do, and none of them dared go home.
sentences.n: “wußten” (1857 for wussten) and “thun” (1857 for tun, ‘to do’) — the ß and the silent -th- run all through this edition. “sich getrauen” = to dare; the verb of motion after heim is dropped (‘dared [go] home’).

sentences.t: Als sie immer nicht zurück kamen, ward der Vater ungeduldig und sprach „gewis haben sies wieder über ein Spiel vergessen, die gottlosen Jungen.“
sentences.l: When they still did not come back, the father grew impatient and said, “surely they have forgotten it again over some game, the godless boys.”
sentences.i: When they still did not come back, the father grew impatient and said, “Surely they have forgotten it again over some game, the wicked boys.”
sentences.n: “ward” is the archaic preterite of werden (‘became, grew’); modern German uses wurde — you will meet ward constantly in Grimm. “gewis” is the 1857 spelling of gewiß. “sies” = sie es (‘they [have forgotten] it’). “kamen zurück” (zurückkommen) is separable.

sentences.t: Es ward ihm angst das Mädchen müßte ungetauft verscheiden und im Ärger rief er „ich wollte daß die Jungen alle zu Raben würden.“
sentences.l: It grew fearful to him [that] the girl might pass away unbaptized, and in his vexation he cried, “I wish that the boys all became ravens.”
sentences.i: He grew afraid the girl would die unbaptized, and in his vexation he cried, “I wish the boys would all turn into ravens!”
sentences.n: “es ward ihm angst” is an impersonal dative idiom, ‘fear came over him.’ “müßte” (1857 for müsste) and “würden” are subjunctive — the reported fear and the fatal wish. “ich wollte daß ...” = ‘I wish that ...’; “daß” is the 1857 spelling of dass.

sentences.t: Kaum war das Wort ausgeredet, so hörte er ein Geschwirr über seinem Haupt in der Luft, blickte in die Höhe und sah sieben kohlschwarze Raben auf und davon fliegen.
sentences.l: Scarcely was the word spoken out, when he heard a whirring above his head in the air, looked up into the height, and saw seven coal-black ravens fly up and away.
sentences.i: The word was scarcely out of his mouth when he heard a whirring above his head in the air, looked up, and saw seven coal-black ravens flying up and away.
sentences.n: “Kaum ... so ...” = ‘scarcely ... when ...’ “ausgeredet” (ausreden, to finish speaking) is separable. “Haupt” is an elevated word for ‘head.’ “auf und davon” is a set phrase, ‘up and away, off and gone.’

sentences.t: Die Eltern konnten die Verwünschung nicht mehr zurücknehmen, und so traurig sie über den Verlust ihrer sieben Söhne waren, trösteten sie sich doch einigermaßen durch ihr liebes Töchterchen, das bald zu Kräften kam, und mit jedem Tage schöner ward.
sentences.l: The parents could no longer take back the curse, and however sad they were over the loss of their seven sons, they yet comforted themselves somewhat through their dear little daughter, who soon came to strength and grew more beautiful with each day.
sentences.i: The parents could no longer take back the curse, and however grieved they were at the loss of their seven sons, they yet found some comfort in their dear little daughter, who soon gained strength and grew fairer with every day.
sentences.n: “so traurig sie ... waren” = ‘however sad they were’ (so + adjective + subject makes a concessive ‘however ...’). “zu Kräften kommen” is an idiom, ‘to gain strength.’ “ward” once more — grew, became.

sentences.t: Es wußte lange Zeit nicht einmal daß es Geschwister gehabt hatte, denn die Eltern hüteten sich ihrer zu erwähnen, bis es eines Tags von ungefähr die Leute von sich sprechen hörte, das Mädchen wäre wohl schön, aber doch eigentlich Schuld an dem Unglück seiner sieben Brüder.
sentences.l: For a long time it did not even know that it had had siblings, for the parents took care not to mention them, until one day it by chance heard the people speak of it — the girl was indeed fair, but really to blame for the misfortune of her seven brothers.
sentences.i: For a long time she did not even know she had had brothers and sisters, for the parents took care not to mention them, until one day she happened to hear people talking of her — that the girl was fair enough, but was really the cause of her seven brothers’ misfortune.
sentences.n: The girl is das Mädchen (neuter), so the German says “es ... seiner” (‘it ... its’) — read them as ‘she ... her.’ “sich hüten, etwas zu tun” = to take care not to do something. “ihrer erwähnen”: erwähnen here governs a genitive object (ihrer, ‘of them’). “von ungefähr” = by chance. “wäre ... Schuld” is subjunctive of reported speech — what the people said.

sentences.t: Da ward es ganz betrübt, gieng zu Vater und Mutter und fragte ob es denn Brüder gehabt hätte und wo sie hingerathen wären?
sentences.l: Then it grew quite grieved, went to father and mother, and asked whether it had had brothers, and where they had got to.
sentences.i: Then she grew quite sorrowful, went to her father and mother, and asked whether she had really had brothers, and where they had gone.
sentences.n: “gieng” is the 1857 spelling of ging (‘went’); this -ie- spelling recurs throughout the edition. “gehabt hätte ... hingerathen wären” are subjunctive of the reported (indirect) question. “hingerathen” (1857 -th-; modern hingeraten) = got to, ended up.

sentences.t: Nun durften die Eltern das Geheimnis nicht länger verschweigen, sagten jedoch es sei so des Himmels Verhängnis und seine Geburt nur der unschuldige Anlaß gewesen.
sentences.l: Now the parents dared no longer keep the secret, but said it was so heaven’s decree, and its birth had been only the innocent occasion.
sentences.i: Now the parents could no longer keep the secret, but said that it was heaven’s own decree, and her birth had only been the innocent occasion of it.
sentences.n: “durften nicht länger” = were no longer to / could no longer bring themselves to. “jedoch” = however, yet. “sei ... gewesen” is subjunctive I of reported speech. “des Himmels Verhängnis” puts the genitive before its noun (‘heaven’s decree’). “Anlaß” is the 1857 spelling of Anlass.

sentences.t: Allein das Mädchen machte sich täglich ein Gewissen daraus und glaubte es müßte seine Geschwister wieder erlösen.
sentences.l: But the girl daily made a conscience of it and believed it must redeem its siblings again.
sentences.i: But the girl daily reproached herself over it, and believed she must set her brothers and sisters free again.
sentences.n: “allein” here has its old sense ‘but, only.’ “sich ein Gewissen aus etwas machen” = to feel scruples about / reproach oneself over something. “müßte ... erlösen” is subjunctive — what she believed she must do.

sentences.t: Es hatte nicht Ruhe und Rast, bis es sich heimlich aufmachte und in die weite Welt gieng, seine Brüder irgendwo aufzuspüren und zu befreien, es möchte kosten was es wollte.
sentences.l: It had no peace and rest, until it secretly set out and went into the wide world, to track down its brothers somewhere and to free them, it might cost what it would.
sentences.i: She had no peace or rest until she secretly set out and went into the wide world to seek out her brothers somewhere and free them, cost what it might.
sentences.n: “Ruhe und Rast” is a fixed pair, ‘peace and rest.’ “sich aufmachen” = to set out. “es möchte kosten was es wollte” is a set phrase, ‘cost what it may’ (subjunctive).

sentences.t: Es nahm nichts mit sich als ein Ringlein von seinen Eltern zum Andenken, einen Laib Brot für den Hunger, ein Krüglein Wasser für den Durst, und ein Stühlchen für die Müdigkeit.
sentences.l: It took nothing with itself but a little ring from its parents as a keepsake, a loaf of bread for the hunger, a little jug of water for the thirst, and a little chair for the weariness.
sentences.i: She took nothing with her but a little ring from her parents as a keepsake, a loaf of bread for hunger, a little jug of water for thirst, and a little stool for weariness.
sentences.n: “nichts ... als” = nothing but. “zum Andenken” = as a keepsake. Note the string of diminutives — Ringlein, Krüglein, Stühlchen — the -lein/-chen endings that pervade this tale, each naming a small, dear thing.

sentences.t: Nun gieng es immer zu, weit weit bis an der Welt Ende.
sentences.l: Now it went ever onward, far, far, right to the world’s end.
sentences.i: Now on she went, ever onward, far, far, to the very end of the world.
sentences.n: “immer zu” = ever onward, straight on (immer + a direction, ‘keep on ...’). “an der Welt Ende” sets the genitive der Welt before Ende — ‘the world’s end.’

sentences.t: Da kam es zur Sonne, aber die war zu heiß und fürchterlich, und fraß die kleinen Kinder.
sentences.l: There it came to the sun, but she was too hot and dreadful, and devoured the little children.
sentences.i: There she came to the sun, but the sun was too hot and terrible, and devoured little children.
sentences.n: “die” here is a demonstrative pointing back to die Sonne (‘she, that one’). “fraß” is the preterite of fressen — the verb used of animals’ or brutish, greedy eating, as against human essen.

sentences.t: Eilig lief es weg und lief hin zu dem Mond, aber der war gar zu kalt und auch grausig und bös, und als er das Kind merkte, sprach er „ich rieche rieche Menschenfleisch.“
sentences.l: Hurriedly it ran away and ran over to the moon, but he was far too cold and also gruesome and wicked, and when he noticed the child, he said, “I smell, smell human flesh.”
sentences.i: In haste she ran away and ran to the moon, but he was far too cold, and gruesome and wicked besides, and when he noticed the child, he said, “I smell, smell the flesh of a man.”
sentences.n: “lief weg” (weglaufen) and “lief hin” (hinlaufen) are both separable. “gar zu” = far too, altogether too. “bös” is the uninflected old form of böse. The doubled “rieche rieche” is the ogre-figure’s menacing repetition — kept as printed.

sentences.t: Da machte es sich geschwind fort und kam zu den Sternen, die waren ihm freundlich und gut, und jeder saß auf seinem besondern Stühlchen.
sentences.l: Then it quickly made itself off and came to the stars, who were friendly and good to it, and each sat on its own particular little chair.
sentences.i: Then she quickly took herself off and came to the stars, who were kind and good to her, and each one sat on its own little chair.
sentences.n: “sich fortmachen” = to make off, get away; “geschwind” = quickly. “die waren ihm freundlich” — the stars were kind to her (dative ihm). “besondern” = besonderen, own/particular.

sentences.t: Der Morgenstern aber stand auf, gab ihm ein Hinkelbeinchen und sprach „wenn du das Beinchen nicht hast, kannst du den Glasberg nicht aufschließen, und in dem Glasberg da sind deine Brüder.“
sentences.l: But the morning star stood up, gave it a little chicken-leg bone, and said, “if you do not have the little bone, you cannot unlock the glass mountain, and in the glass mountain, there are your brothers.”
sentences.i: But the morning star rose up, gave her a little chicken bone, and said, “If you do not have this little bone, you cannot unlock the glass mountain, and it is in the glass mountain that your brothers are.”
sentences.n: “stand auf” (aufstehen) and “aufschließen” (to unlock) are separable in auf-. “Hinkelbeinchen”: Hinkel is a dialect word for a hen/chicken, so a little chicken-bone. This bone is the story’s magic key.

sentences.t: Das Mädchen nahm das Beinchen, wickelte es wohl in ein Tüchlein, und gieng wieder fort so lange bis es an den Glasberg kam.
sentences.l: The girl took the little bone, wrapped it well in a little cloth, and went away again so long until it came to the glass mountain.
sentences.i: The girl took the little bone, wrapped it carefully in a little cloth, and went on again until she came to the glass mountain.
sentences.n: “wohl” here means ‘carefully, well.’ “gieng fort” (fortgehen) is separable. “so lange bis” = until (literally ‘so long until’).

sentences.t: Das Thor war verschlossen und es wollte das Beinchen hervor holen, aber wie es das Tüchlein aufmachte, so war es leer, und es hatte das Geschenk der guten Sterne verloren.
sentences.l: The gate was locked and it wanted to fetch out the little bone, but as it opened the little cloth, it was empty, and it had lost the gift of the good stars.
sentences.i: The gate was locked, and she went to take out the little bone; but when she opened the little cloth, it was empty — she had lost the good stars’ gift.
sentences.n: “Thor” is the 1857 spelling of Tor (‘gate’), with the old -th-. “hervor holen” (hervorholen, to fetch out) and “aufmachte” (aufmachen, to open) are separable. “wie” here = ‘as, when.’

sentences.t: Was sollte es nun anfangen?
sentences.l: What should it now begin?
sentences.i: What was she to do now?
sentences.n: “etwas anfangen” here means ‘to do, undertake’ — “was anfangen?” is the idiom ‘what to do?’, not literally ‘what to begin?’

sentences.t: seine Brüder wollte es erretten und hatte keinen Schlüssel zum Glasberg.
sentences.l: Its brothers it wanted to rescue, and had no key to the glass mountain.
sentences.i: She wanted to save her brothers, and had no key to the glass mountain.
sentences.n: The clause is printed with a small initial letter, running straight on from the question before it. Fronting the object “seine Brüder” throws the weight onto what she means to rescue.

sentences.t: Das gute Schwesterchen nahm ein Messer, schnitt sich ein kleines Fingerchen ab, steckte es in das Thor und schloß glücklich auf.
sentences.l: The good little sister took a knife, cut off a little finger for herself, stuck it into the gate, and unlocked it happily.
sentences.i: The good little sister took a knife, cut off one of her little fingers, put it into the gate, and successfully unlocked it.
sentences.n: “schnitt ... ab” (abschneiden) and “schloß auf” (aufschließen, to unlock) are separable — schloß is the 1857 spelling of schloss. “glücklich” here = ‘luckily, successfully.’ The tale does not soften this: she cuts off her own finger to serve as a key.

sentences.t: Als es eingegangen war, kam ihm ein Zwerglein entgegen, das sprach „mein Kind, was suchst du?“
sentences.l: When it had entered, a little dwarf came toward it, who said, “my child, what do you seek?”
sentences.i: When she had gone in, a little dwarf came to meet her and said, “My child, what are you looking for?”
sentences.n: “eingegangen” (eingehen, to enter) is separable. “kam entgegen” (entgegenkommen, to come to meet) takes a dative (ihm) — ‘came toward her.’

sentences.t: „Ich suche meine Brüder, die sieben Raben,“ antwortete es.
sentences.l: “I seek my brothers, the seven ravens,” it answered.
sentences.i: “I am looking for my brothers, the seven ravens,” she answered.

sentences.t: Der Zwerg sprach „die Herren Raben sind nicht zu Haus, aber willst du hier so lang warten, bis sie kommen, so tritt ein.“
sentences.l: The dwarf said, “the lord ravens are not at home, but if you will wait here so long until they come, then step in.”
sentences.i: The dwarf said, “The master ravens are not at home, but if you will wait here until they come, then step inside.”
sentences.n: “die Herren Raben” — Herren (‘lords, gentlemen’) before an animal name is a mock-courteous ‘their lordships the ravens.’ “willst du ..., so ...” is a conditional made by verb-first inversion (‘if you will ..., then ...’). “tritt ein” (eintreten, to step in) is a separable imperative.

sentences.t: Darauf trug das Zwerglein die Speise der Raben herein auf sieben Tellerchen und in sieben Becherchen, und von jedem Tellerchen aß das Schwesterchen ein Bröckchen, und aus jedem Becherchen trank es ein Schlückchen; in das letzte Becherchen aber ließ es das Ringlein fallen, das es mitgenommen hatte.
sentences.l: Thereupon the little dwarf carried in the ravens’ food on seven little plates and in seven little cups, and from each little plate the little sister ate a little morsel, and from each little cup it drank a little sip; but into the last little cup it let the little ring fall that it had taken along.
sentences.i: Thereupon the little dwarf carried in the ravens’ meal on seven little plates and in seven little cups, and from each little plate the little sister ate a morsel, and from each little cup she drank a sip; but into the last cup she let fall the little ring she had brought with her.
sentences.n: “trug ... herein” (hereintragen, to carry in) is separable. Note the swarm of diminutives (Tellerchen, Becherchen, Bröckchen, Schlückchen, Ringlein) — this piling-up of tiny things is a signature of the tale. “ließ ... fallen” = let fall, dropped.

sentences.t: Auf einmal hörte es in der Luft ein Geschwirr und ein Geweh, da sprach das Zwerglein „jetzt kommen die Herren Raben heim geflogen.“
sentences.l: All at once it heard in the air a whirring and a wailing, then the little dwarf said, “now the lord ravens come flying home.”
sentences.i: All at once she heard a whirring and a wailing in the air, and the little dwarf said, “Now the master ravens come flying home.”
sentences.n: “auf einmal” = all at once. “Geschwirr” (a whirring, of wings) and “Geweh” (a wailing, a rare collective of Weh, ‘woe’): the ge-…-noun here names a continuous, collective sound. “kommen ... heim geflogen” combines heimkommen with the participle geflogen — ‘come flying home.’

sentences.t: Da kamen sie, wollten essen und trinken, und suchten ihre Tellerchen und Becherchen.
sentences.l: Then they came, wanted to eat and drink, and looked for their little plates and little cups.
sentences.i: Then they came, meaning to eat and drink, and looked for their little plates and cups.

sentences.t: Da sprach einer nach dem andern „wer hat von meinem Tellerchen gegessen? wer hat aus meinem Becherchen getrunken? das ist eines Menschen Mund gewesen.“
sentences.l: Then one after another said, “who has eaten from my little plate? who has drunk from my little cup? that has been a human’s mouth.”
sentences.i: Then one after another said, “Who has been eating from my little plate? Who has been drinking from my little cup? That was a human mouth.”
sentences.n: “einer nach dem andern” = one after another. “eines Menschen Mund” — the genitive eines Menschen (‘a human being’s’) stands before its noun. The three-fold question echoes the fairy-tale pattern of the intruder found out.

sentences.t: Und wie der siebente auf den Grund des Bechers kam, rollte ihm das Ringlein entgegen.
sentences.l: And as the seventh came to the bottom of the cup, the little ring rolled toward him.
sentences.i: And when the seventh reached the bottom of his cup, the little ring rolled out to meet him.
sentences.n: “wie” = as, when. “rollte ihm ... entgegen” (entgegenrollen, to roll toward) takes the dative ihm — rolled ‘to meet him.’

sentences.t: Da sah er es an und erkannte daß es ein Ring von Vater und Mutter war, und sprach „Gott gebe, unser Schwesterlein wäre da, so wären wir erlöst.“
sentences.l: Then he looked at it and recognized that it was a ring of father and mother, and said, “God grant our little sister were here, then we should be redeemed.”
sentences.i: Then he looked at it and knew it for a ring of father and mother, and said, “God grant our little sister were here — then we should be set free.”
sentences.n: “sah ... an” (ansehen, to look at) is separable. “Gott gebe” — gebe is present subjunctive, ‘God grant, may God grant.’ “wäre ... wären” are subjunctive II voicing the longed-for, unreal wish (‘were she here, we should be freed’).

sentences.t: Wie das Mädchen, das hinter der Thüre stand und lauschte, den Wunsch hörte, so trat es hervor, und da bekamen alle die Raben ihre menschliche Gestalt wieder.
sentences.l: As the girl, who stood behind the door and listened, heard the wish, so it stepped forth, and then all the ravens got their human form again.
sentences.i: When the girl, who stood behind the door listening, heard the wish, she stepped forth, and at once all the ravens got their human shape again.
sentences.n: “Thüre” is the 1857 spelling of Türe/Tür (‘door’), with the old -th-. “trat hervor” (hervortreten, to step forth) is separable. “bekamen ... wieder” — wiederbekommen, ‘to get back.’

sentences.t: Und sie herzten und küßten einander, und zogen fröhlich heim.
sentences.l: And they hugged and kissed one another, and went merrily home.
sentences.i: And they embraced and kissed one another, and went joyfully home.
sentences.n: “herzen” = to embrace, hold to one’s heart (from Herz). “küßten” is the 1857 spelling of küssten. “zogen heim” (heimziehen, to journey home) is separable — ziehen of travelling. A brisk fairy-tale close.
